name: 23andMe
summary: Pioneering consumer genetic-testing company faced a major 2023 data breach and never found a profitable business beyond one-time DNA kits, filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in March 2025.
lesson: A one-time-purchase consumer product needs a recurring revenue engine to survive beyond the initial hype cycle.

name: 2U Inc.
summary: Online program management company that powered university degrees and acquired edX filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after years of stock decline and unsustainable university revenue-share contracts.
lesson: Revenue-share contracts with universities can become structurally unprofitable as the market matures.

name: 5miles
summary: Local classifieds and secondhand marketplace app raised heavy funding but was shut down around 2019 after failing to compete with letgo and OfferUp.
lesson: Heavily funded but undifferentiated marketplace apps struggle once category leaders consolidate share.

name: A123 Systems
summary: MIT-spinoff lithium-ion battery maker won a $249M DOE grant but was crushed by a costly Fisker recall and cheap competition, filing for bankruptcy in 2012.
lesson: Battery makers are highly exposed to the fortunes of the small number of automaker customers they depend on.

name: Akili Interactive
summary: Maker of EndeavorRx, the first FDA-cleared video-game-based digital therapeutic for ADHD, went public via SPAC but struggled commercially and sold its core assets in 2024 after running low on cash.
lesson: FDA clearance alone doesn't guarantee payer coverage or consumer willingness to pay.

name: Amazon Care
summary: Amazon's in-house telehealth and home-visit service for employees and enterprise clients was shut down in 2022 as Amazon pivoted its healthcare strategy toward acquiring One Medical.
lesson: Even deep-pocketed corporate backers will kill a healthcare bet that doesn't show a path to scale.

name: Anki
summary: Maker of consumer robotics toys shut down abruptly after failing to close a final funding round, despite selling over 1.5 million robots.
lesson: Consumer robotics toy margins can be too thin to sustain the R&D costs of building lovable hardware.

name: Argo AI
summary: Self-driving AI startup backed by Ford and Volkswagen shut down after both automakers concluded fully autonomous robotaxi tech was too far from commercial viability.
lesson: Deep-pocketed corporate backers can still walk away when a capital-intensive AI bet's timeline stretches too long.

name: Arrival
summary: UK-based electric van and bus maker known for its 'microfactory' manufacturing concept that went public via SPAC in 2021 but filed for bankruptcy in 2024 after failing to reach production at scale.
lesson: Novel micro-factory manufacturing concepts still need to prove out at commercial volume before investors' patience runs out.

name: Arrivo
summary: Hyperloop-adjacent high-speed ground transportation startup founded by a former Hyperloop One executive that shut down in 2018 after failing to secure a route deal in Colorado.
lesson: Infrastructure-dependent transportation startups need a committed government partner before spending down capital on engineering.

name: Azlo
summary: No-fee digital banking app for freelancers and small businesses, backed by BBVA, that shut down in 2021 after BBVA sold its US banking operations to PNC and discontinued the unprofitable unit.
lesson: A fintech dependent on a single incumbent-bank sponsor is exposed to that sponsor's own strategic pivots.

name: b8ta
summary: Retail-as-a-service startup that let D2C brands showcase products in physical stores shut down its US retail operations in 2023 after failing to reach profitability.
lesson: Bridging online D2C brands into physical retail carries real estate and staffing costs that are hard to offset.

name: Babylon Health
summary: UK-based AI-driven telehealth and symptom-checker company expanded aggressively into US value-based care contracts, burned through cash, and filed for bankruptcy/liquidation in 2023.
lesson: Scaling into risk-bearing healthcare contracts without disciplined unit economics can sink even well-funded tech.

name: Beachmint
summary: Celebrity-branded subscription commerce startup spanning fashion and beauty brands shut down in 2015 after its multi-brand strategy failed to gain traction.
lesson: Launching multiple celebrity-branded subscription lines simultaneously spreads resources too thin to build any one brand well.

name: Beepi
summary: Peer-to-peer used car buying and selling marketplace shut down in 2017 after burning through heavy funding without solving the capital intensity of buying and reconditioning cars.
lesson: Used-car marketplaces require far more working capital than typical marketplace models assume.

name: Bench Accounting
summary: Online bookkeeping and tax platform for small businesses that abruptly shut down in December 2024, locking out customers from their financial records before its assets were acquired by Employer.com.
lesson: Abrupt shutdowns of financial recordkeeping services can cause disproportionate harm to small-business customers relative to the company's own losses.

name: Berkshire Grey
summary: Warehouse robotics and fulfillment automation company that went public via SPAC at a rich valuation, then was taken private by SoftBank at a fraction of that price.
lesson: A SPAC valuation built on future promise can collapse fast once real-world adoption lags.

name: Better Place
summary: Israeli startup built battery-swap stations and EVs but high infrastructure costs and weak vehicle sales led to bankruptcy.
lesson: Building a proprietary infrastructure network before demand exists is extraordinarily expensive and risky.

name: Better.com
summary: Digital mortgage lender that grew rapidly during the pandemic refinancing boom saw its reputation and finances collapse after its CEO fired 900 employees over a Zoom call.
lesson: Scaling headcount to match a temporary rate-driven demand spike leaves a company badly overextended once rates rise.

name: Birchbox
summary: Pioneer of the beauty subscription box model struggled for years with churn and cash shortfalls, laying off large portions of staff repeatedly before being sold in a distressed 2023 deal.
lesson: Subscription box novelty fades fast once retention costs exceed customer lifetime value.

name: Bird
summary: Electric scooter-sharing pioneer that scaled rapidly across hundreds of cities but never achieved sustainable unit economics; filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in December 2023.
lesson: Rapid geographic expansion without solving vehicle durability and repair costs burns cash faster than growth can offset.

name: Bitconnect
summary: Crypto lending platform promising outsized guaranteed returns via a proprietary trading bot shut down after regulators issued cease-and-desist orders, revealing a Ponzi scheme worth over $2B in losses.
lesson: Guaranteed fixed returns with an opaque, unaudited strategy are the classic signature of a Ponzi scheme.

name: BlockFi
summary: Crypto lender filed for Chapter 11 after its exposure to FTX/Alameda, which had bailed it out earlier that year, became fatal.
lesson: Accepting a rescue loan from a counterparty that is itself fragile just delays and compounds the eventual failure.

name: BloomTech (Lambda School)
summary: Coding bootcamp built on income-share agreements that faced state regulatory actions and an FTC investigation over misleading job-placement statistics, shrinking dramatically.
lesson: Income-share-agreement business models invite regulatory scrutiny if outcome claims aren't rigorously verified.

name: Blue Apron
summary: Meal-kit subscription pioneer that IPO'd in 2017, struggled with customer acquisition costs and churn for years, and was acquired by Wonder Group in 2023 after its stock collapsed.
lesson: High customer acquisition costs paired with poor retention doom subscription commerce even with first-mover advantage.

name: Bluefly
summary: Early online discount fashion retailer filed for bankruptcy in 2018 after two decades of struggling to turn a sustainable profit against larger competitors.
lesson: Longevity alone doesn't protect a discount ecommerce retailer without a durable cost or sourcing advantage.

name: Bó
summary: Digital-only bank launched by RBS (NatWest) in late 2019 to compete with Monzo and Starling; shut down just six months later in mid-2020 after failing to gain traction and to justify its cost amid COVID-19.
lesson: An incumbent bank spinning up a challenger brand still needs genuine differentiation, not just a new app skin.

name: Bollinger Motors
summary: Electric truck and commercial van startup that filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy liquidation in 2024 after years of delayed production and failed partnership attempts, including with Mullen Automotive.
lesson: Commercial EV makers need deep-pocketed manufacturing partners; failed partnership talks can be a fatal blow.

name: Bolt Financial
summary: One-click checkout SaaS reached an $11B valuation before layoffs and public controversy over founder claims about funding and metrics led to a collapse in its valuation.
lesson: Founder credibility problems can destroy enterprise sales momentum even when the core product works.

name: Bonobos
summary: Menswear D2C ecommerce pioneer was acquired by Walmart in 2017 for $310M, sold to Express in 2023, and its inventory was liquidated after Express filed for bankruptcy in 2024.
lesson: A strong D2C brand can still be undone by a series of ownership changes that never find it a stable strategic home.

name: Boo.com
summary: European luxury fashion ecommerce site spent lavishly on a Flash-heavy website and global launch before collapsing in 2000, becoming a symbol of dot-com excess.
lesson: Simultaneous global launch and heavy pre-revenue marketing spend is extremely high risk.

name: Bossa Nova Robotics
summary: Retail shelf-scanning robotics company whose flagship deployment with Walmart ended, after which the company ceased its retail-robotics operations.
lesson: Depending on a single anchor retail customer leaves a hardware startup dangerously exposed to one contract decision.

name: Bounce
summary: Indian dockless scooter-rental startup (formerly Metro Bikes) that shut down its core rental business in 2022 to pivot fully into electric-vehicle manufacturing.
lesson: Two-wheeler rental economics in price-sensitive markets pushed the company to abandon its original business entirely.

name: Boxed
summary: Bulk wholesale ecommerce retailer aimed at non-Costco-member shoppers went public via SPAC in 2022 and filed for bankruptcy in 2024, with assets acquired by rival Sam's Club owner.
lesson: Thin-margin bulk retail online struggles to outrun warehouse club incumbents with superior logistics scale.

name: Brandless
summary: D2C consumer goods retailer selling unbranded household products at flat $3 prices; shut down in 2020 after burning through massive SoftBank-led funding without reaching profitability.
lesson: A minimalist branding gimmick cannot substitute for a durable unit-economics advantage.

name: Bridj
summary: On-demand shuttle bus startup piloted in Boston, Kansas City and Sydney that shut down in 2017 after failing to find a sustainable model and losing a major transit-agency partnership.
lesson: Public transit-agency partnerships can be a lifeline or a liability depending on how dependent the startup's revenue is on them.

name: Bright Health
summary: Tech-enabled health insurance startup expanded rapidly into ACA and Medicare Advantage markets nationwide, then exited nearly all insurance lines and sold its remaining businesses by 2024 after massive losses.
lesson: Underwriting risk in health insurance punishes growth-at-all-costs expansion into new geographies and plan types.

name: Builder.ai
summary: AI-powered app-development platform once valued at $1.5B collapsed into insolvency amid revelations that much of its 'AI' output relied on human engineers.
lesson: Overstating AI automation capability to investors and customers eventually collapses under financial scrutiny.

name: Bulletin
summary: Wholesale marketplace connecting independent brands with retail shelf space shut down in 2020 as the pandemic collapsed brick-and-mortar retail demand.
lesson: A marketplace tied to physical retail placement is acutely vulnerable to shocks in offline retail.

name: Byju's
summary: Once the world's most valuable edtech company, Byju's collapsed under aggressive debt-fueled acquisitions, accounting irregularities, and auditor/board resignations, leading to insolvency proceedings.
lesson: Aggressive debt-fueled M&A without financial controls can sink even category-leading startups.

name: Byte
summary: Vine co-creator Dom Hofmann's short-video app reboot launched to fanfare in 2020 but couldn't compete with TikTok's scale and was shut down in 2023.
lesson: Nostalgia for a beloved defunct product doesn't overcome a much larger incumbent's head start.

name: Byton
summary: China-based EV startup founded by ex-BMW and Nissan executives that showcased a futuristic SUV concept for years but never reached mass production and formally dissolved in 2023.
lesson: Years of flashy concept demos can't substitute for an actual production line and delivered vehicles.

name: Calibrate
summary: Telehealth weight-loss startup that combined coaching with GLP-1 prescriptions shut down in 2024 after larger competitors and direct-to-consumer GLP-1 access eroded its differentiation.
lesson: A crowded, fast-moving category can commoditize a startup's advantage within a couple of years.

name: Canoo
summary: EV startup known for its modular 'skateboard' platform that went public via SPAC in 2020, repeatedly restructured, and filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy in 2025 after years of production delays and cash shortfalls.
lesson: A distinctive platform design doesn't substitute for the manufacturing capital needed to actually build cars at scale.

name: Car2Go / Share Now
summary: Daimler's free-floating car-sharing service merged with BMW's DriveNow to form Share Now in 2019, exited North America the same year, and the whole venture shut down in early 2024.
lesson: Free-floating car-sharing fleets require fleet-scale capital that even automakers found unsustainable long term.

name: CarLotz
summary: Consignment-based used car marketplace went public via SPAC in 2021 and shut down operations in 2023 after failing to reach profitability.
lesson: A consignment marketplace model for cars still carries heavy inventory and reconditioning costs.

name: Casper
summary: Bed-in-a-box mattress D2C pioneer IPO'd in 2020 at a fraction of its earlier private valuation, then was taken private in 2022 by Durational Capital at a steep discount.
lesson: A category creator can still lose once dozens of well-funded copycats commoditize the product.

name: Castlight Health
summary: Health benefits navigation and price-transparency company had a blockbuster 2014 IPO but its stock collapsed over 95% amid weak growth, and it was acquired by Vera Whole Health in 2022 for a fraction of its former valuation.
lesson: A hot IPO can mask a business model that never found strong enough retention or pricing power.

name: Celsius Network
summary: Crypto lending platform promising high-yield interest on deposits froze withdrawals and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after risky DeFi bets left it insolvent.
lesson: Yields that outpace any plausible underlying return are usually funded by new depositors, not real profit.

name: Cerebral
summary: Fast-growing telehealth startup for ADHD and mental health prescriptions faced a DOJ investigation into controlled-substance prescribing practices, was cut off by pharmacy partners, and laid off the large majority of its staff in 2022–2023, surviving only in drastically reduced form.
lesson: Growth built on liberal controlled-substance prescribing invites regulatory intervention that can gut a company almost overnight.

name: Chariot
summary: Crowdsourced commuter shuttle-van startup acquired by Ford in 2016 that was shut down in 2019 as Ford Smart Mobility cut underperforming units amid a broader restructuring.
lesson: Shuttle-van services face brutal per-seat economics that scale poorly even under automaker ownership.

name: Chegg
summary: Homework-help platform's stock collapsed over 99% from its 2021 peak after the CEO disclosed that ChatGPT was materially eroding subscriber growth, triggering mass layoffs.
lesson: Generative AI can rapidly commoditize a business built on giving students step-by-step answers.

name: Circ
summary: Berlin-based e-scooter startup (rebranded from Flash) acquired by Bird in 2020 as the European scooter market consolidated rapidly.
lesson: In a saturated market, being acquired by a larger rival is often the only viable exit.

name: Close5
summary: eBay's local classifieds marketplace app aimed at competing with letgo and OfferUp was shut down in 2017 after failing to gain sufficient market share.
lesson: Launching a me-too marketplace app into an already-crowded category rarely overtakes established leaders.

name: Clover Health
summary: Medicare Advantage insurtech taken public via a Chamath Palihapitiya SPAC in 2021; its stock collapsed over 90% amid short-seller allegations, an SEC inquiry, and persistent medical-cost losses.
lesson: SPAC hype cycles can inflate a health-insurance startup's valuation well beyond what its loss ratios can support.

name: Coda Automotive
summary: Los Angeles-based EV sedan startup that sold fewer than 100 cars before filing for bankruptcy in 2013 due to poor product quality and inability to raise further capital.
lesson: A poorly reviewed debut product can doom an EV startup before it ever reaches meaningful volume.

name: Convoy
summary: Digital freight brokerage once valued at $3.8B that shut down after a slowing freight market and a failed sale process.
lesson: Even well-funded marketplaces can't outrun a cyclical downturn without a path to profitability.

name: Coord
summary: Curb-space and parking-data startup spun out of Sidewalk Labs that wound down in 2022 after failing to build a large enough commercial base for city curb-management data.
lesson: City infrastructure data products can have long sales cycles that outlast a startup's runway.

name: Craftsy
summary: Online marketplace and classes platform for crafting supplies and instruction was sold off in pieces starting in 2018, with the original platform ceasing operations by 2019.
lesson: Bundling ecommerce and content/education products can dilute focus on either business.

name: Cruise
summary: GM's robotaxi subsidiary suspended all driverless operations nationwide in late 2023 after a pedestrian dragging incident in San Francisco, leading to major layoffs, leadership departures, and GM later halting robotaxi development entirely in 2024.
lesson: A single serious safety incident can unravel years of regulatory goodwill and force a strategic retreat from autonomy.

name: Cue Health
summary: Maker of at-home molecular COVID-19 test devices boomed during the pandemic on government contracts but collapsed as testing demand evaporated, laying off nearly all staff and winding down operations in 2024.
lesson: Demand pulled forward by a crisis can vanish just as fast, stranding capacity built for peak conditions.

name: Curbside
summary: Mobile curbside pickup and local commerce platform partnering with retailers shut down in 2019 after failing to build a sustainable standalone business.
lesson: Middleman commerce infrastructure is vulnerable once retailers build the same capability in-house.

name: Deliv
summary: Crowdsourced same-day delivery startup partnering with retailers and malls shut down in 2020 as the pandemic and competition from larger logistics players squeezed it out.
lesson: Last-mile logistics is a scale game that favors deep-pocketed incumbents over asset-light middlemen.

name: Desktop Metal
summary: Industrial metal 3D-printing manufacturer went public via SPAC, made a string of acquisitions, then saw its stock collapse and a planned merger with Stratasys collapse amid mounting losses.
lesson: Rolling up multiple acquisitions to build a manufacturing platform can multiply integration and cash-burn risk instead of synergies.

name: Digg
summary: The pioneering social news aggregator was overtaken by Reddit and Facebook after a disastrous 2010 redesign, and was sold for parts for about $500,000 in 2012.
lesson: A poorly executed redesign can destroy years of community trust almost overnight.

name: Divvy Homes
summary: Rent-to-own real estate startup was acquired by Brookfield Properties in 2024 at roughly half its peak $2.3B valuation, after rising rates hurt its model.
lesson: Balance-sheet-heavy rent-to-own models are highly exposed to interest rate cycles.

name: Docker Inc
summary: Container technology pioneer whose developer tool became an industry standard, but whose enterprise sales business failed to generate proportional revenue; sold its enterprise unit to Mirantis.
lesson: Ubiquitous open-source adoption doesn't guarantee a company can capture commensurate enterprise revenue.

name: Dot & Bo
summary: Curated furniture and home goods ecommerce startup shut down in 2017 after failing to overcome the high shipping costs and returns typical of furniture ecommerce.
lesson: Furniture ecommerce margins are easily wiped out by freight and return logistics.

name: Drive.ai
summary: Self-driving startup known for its distinctive orange vans and Texas robotaxi pilots that ran low on funding and was acquired by Apple in 2019 mainly for engineering talent.
lesson: A visible pilot program isn't the same as a fundable path to commercial-scale autonomy.

name: eBags
summary: Online luggage and bags retailer was acquired by Samsonite in 2017 and shut down in 2020 amid pandemic-driven travel collapse and parent-company struggles.
lesson: A niche ecommerce category tied closely to travel is acutely exposed to macro demand shocks.

name: Electric Last Mile Solutions
summary: Electric delivery van maker that went public via SPAC in 2021 but filed for bankruptcy just over a year later in 2022 amid an accounting scandal over founder stock issuances.
lesson: SPAC-era EV startups with weak governance controls were especially vulnerable to founder self-dealing scandals.

name: Embark Trucks
summary: Publicly-traded autonomous trucking startup that shut down operations and sold off its assets and IP in 2023 after running out of runway to reach commercial deployment.
lesson: Long AV development timelines routinely outlast the funding cycles available to sustain them.

name: eToys.com
summary: Online toy retailer that briefly outvalued Toys R Us during the dot-com boom filed for bankruptcy in 2001 after overspending on marketing and logistics infrastructure.
lesson: A high stock valuation is not a substitute for a profitable operating model.

name: Evernote
summary: Pioneering notes app once reached a $1B+ valuation but suffered years of stagnation and declining relevance against Notion, culminating in its 2023 sale to Bending Spoons.
lesson: Early category leadership can erode without continued product innovation and focus.

name: Fab.com
summary: Design flash-sale ecommerce site that raised huge funding at a $1B+ valuation, pivoted repeatedly, then sold off assets for a fraction of capital raised.
lesson: Scaling headcount and international expansion ahead of a proven business model invites collapse.

name: Fair.com
summary: Flexible car-leasing/subscription startup led by former Uber and CreditKarma executives that laid off most of its staff in 2019 and effectively collapsed after burning through massive debt financing.
lesson: Using large amounts of debt to subsidize a subscription vehicle model magnifies losses when demand assumptions are wrong.

name: Faraday Future
summary: China-backed luxury EV startup that promised to rival Tesla but has produced only a tiny number of its FF 91 vehicles amid years of near-bankruptcy, executive turnover, and fraud allegations from the SEC.
lesson: Grandiose promises without manufacturing discipline erode investor and consumer trust long before a product ships at scale.

name: Farmigo
summary: Farm-to-table grocery marketplace connecting local farmers to consumers shut down its consumer marketplace in 2018 due to unsustainable logistics costs.
lesson: Hyperlocal food logistics networks are extremely expensive to operate at consumer-marketplace scale.

name: Farmstead
summary: AI-driven online grocery delivery startup shut down in 2020 after failing to scale its warehouse model profitably.
lesson: Grocery delivery margins remain thin even with software-driven inventory optimization.

name: Fast
summary: One-click checkout startup for ecommerce sites shut down abruptly in 2022 after burning through funding faster than it could sign up merchants, despite a high-profile launch.
lesson: Infrastructure startups need real merchant adoption metrics, not just hype, before scaling spend.

name: Fast (Fast.co)
summary: One-click checkout startup that shut down abruptly in April 2022 despite recent large fundraising, after reports surfaced of minimal revenue, poor merchant adoption, and unsustainable spending.
lesson: Impressive fundraising totals can mask an absence of real merchant demand for a product.

name: Fast Radius
summary: On-demand digital manufacturing company went public via SPAC then filed for bankruptcy after failing to control costs.
lesson: SPAC-funded manufacturing companies with heavy fixed costs need real revenue traction to survive as public companies.

name: Fernish
summary: Furniture rental startup for apartment dwellers shut down in 2022 after failing to raise a new funding round amid tightening venture capital.
lesson: Asset-heavy rental commerce models need continuous capital access that dries up quickly in downturns.

name: First Republic Bank
summary: Private bank serving high-net-worth clients that was seized by regulators and sold to JPMorgan in May 2023 after a wave of uninsured deposit withdrawals exposed large unrealized losses on low-rate mortgages.
lesson: Long-duration, low-yield assets funded by short-term deposits become a solvency risk once interest rates rise sharply.

name: Fisker Automotive
summary: Luxury plug-in hybrid maker, backed by a $529M DOE loan, filed for bankruptcy after battery supplier A123's collapse and recalls.
lesson: Startups building complex hardware are exposed to their suppliers' failures, not just their own execution.

name: Fisker Automotive (1st company)
summary: Henrik Fisker's first EV company built the Karma plug-in hybrid but collapsed into bankruptcy in 2013 after battery supplier A123's bankruptcy, manufacturing delays, and a government loan default.
lesson: Overreliance on a single battery supplier creates existential single-point-of-failure risk for an EV startup.

name: Fisker Inc. (2nd company)
summary: Henrik Fisker's second EV venture launched the Ocean SUV but filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in June 2024 after severe production quality issues, plunging sales, and inability to raise further capital.
lesson: Rushing a complex vehicle to market without a mature manufacturing and quality process leads to a death spiral of recalls and returns.

name: FlightCar
summary: Airport car-sharing startup letting travelers rent out their cars while away that shut down in 2017 amid insurance disputes, lawsuits, and unsustainable operating losses.
lesson: Insurance and liability complexity in peer-to-peer vehicle sharing can create legal exposure that outpaces revenue.

name: Forward Health
summary: Concierge primary-care startup founded by ex-Google exec Adrian Aoun built high-tech physical clinics and later automated 'CarePods'; it shut down operations after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: High-touch, capital-intensive physical clinics are extremely expensive to scale nationally.

name: Frank
summary: Financial-aid application startup sold to JPMorgan Chase for $175M in 2021; JPMorgan later sued founder Charlie Javice alleging she fabricated millions of fake user accounts to inflate the sale price.
lesson: Acquirers must independently verify user metrics rather than relying on target-provided data during diligence.

name: Friday Health Plans
summary: Tech-enabled ACA marketplace health insurance startup expanded into multiple states quickly, then became insolvent in 2023, with regulators in several states placing it into liquidation.
lesson: Underpricing insurance risk while expanding into new state markets can trigger simultaneous, catastrophic losses.

name: FTX
summary: Once the world's second-largest crypto exchange, FTX collapsed in November 2022 after Alameda Research's balance sheet was exposed as built on FTX's own illiquid token.
lesson: Commingling customer funds with a trading affiliate is fraud, not a business model.

name: Fyre Media (Fyre Festival)
summary: Luxury music festival venture collapsed spectacularly when the promised Bahamas festival turned out to be built on fraudulent marketing, leading to the founder's imprisonment.
lesson: Marketing hype divorced from operational reality in event/travel experiences can constitute outright fraud, not just failure.

name: Garden.com
summary: Online gardening supplies retailer shut down in 2001 after the dot-com downturn dried up funding for its niche, low-margin category.
lesson: Niche ecommerce categories with thin margins are especially exposed when capital markets tighten.

name: Genesis Global Capital
summary: Major institutional crypto lender owned by Digital Currency Group suspended withdrawals after the FTX collapse exposed its exposure and filed for Chapter 11.
lesson: Institutional lenders are only as sound as the riskiest counterparty in their loan book.

name: Getaround
summary: Peer-to-peer car-sharing marketplace that went public via SPAC in 2022 at a rich valuation but saw its stock collapse over 99%, leading to a Nasdaq delisting in 2024.
lesson: A SPAC listing can mask unresolved unit-economics problems that eventually surface in public markets.

name: Getir
summary: Istanbul-founded rapid grocery delivery pioneer that expanded aggressively into the US, UK and Germany, acquiring Gorillas and FreshDirect, then retreated from all three markets in 2023.
lesson: Aggressive international expansion in quick-commerce outpaced the ability to reach profitability anywhere.

name: Gilt Groupe
summary: Flash-sale luxury ecommerce pioneer once valued over $1B was sold to Hudson's Bay for $250M in 2016 and wound down within a few years as flash sales fell out of favor.
lesson: A trend-driven business model can lose relevance quickly once consumer shopping habits shift.

name: Google+
summary: Google's multibillion-dollar bid to challenge Facebook never achieved meaningful organic engagement and was shut down in 2019 following a data-exposure bug.
lesson: Forcing adoption across a company's other products cannot manufacture genuine social engagement.

name: Gorillas
summary: Berlin rapid grocery delivery pioneer that scaled to dozens of cities in under two years before being acquired by rival Getir in a distressed down-round deal.
lesson: Hypergrowth in 10-minute grocery delivery outran the ability to reach profitable dark-store economics.

name: Gotcha Mobility
summary: South Carolina-based micromobility and car-share operator serving smaller college towns that ceased operations in 2023 as funding dried up.
lesson: Serving smaller secondary markets doesn't insulate a mobility startup from the same capital constraints as big-city rivals.

name: GreenSky
summary: Point-of-sale home-improvement lender acquired by Goldman Sachs in 2021 for $2.24B as part of its consumer banking push; Goldman sold it to Sixth Street in 2023 for roughly a quarter of that price as it retreated from consumer lending.
lesson: Even a well-established fintech can become a write-down when its acquirer's own strategic priorities change.

name: Groupon
summary: Daily deals pioneer IPO'd in 2011 at a multi-billion-dollar valuation but its stock lost over 99% of its value as the deals model proved unsustainable and merchants churned.
lesson: A viral growth model built on one-time deal customers rarely produces durable repeat revenue.

name: Grow (Grin + Yellow)
summary: Latin American bike and scooter-sharing giant formed by the merger of Brazil's Yellow and Mexico's Grin that shut down operations across the region in 2020 amid the COVID-19 demand collapse and cash shortages.
lesson: Merging two cash-burning competitors doesn't fix the underlying unit economics, especially heading into a demand shock.

name: Grubhub
summary: Early online food ordering and delivery pioneer IPO'd in 2014 but was sold by Just Eat Takeaway to Wonder Group in 2024 for about $650M, a steep loss from the $7.3B acquisition price paid in 2021.
lesson: Market share leadership in food delivery erodes fast without continual reinvestment against better-funded rivals.

name: HauteLook
summary: Flash-sale fashion ecommerce site was acquired by Nordstrom in 2011 for $180M and was folded into Nordstrom Rack before being shut down in 2021.
lesson: Flash-sale ecommerce formats can lose relevance even under a well-capitalized retail parent.

name: Helbiz
summary: NYC-based micromobility company that went public via SPAC in 2021, acquired multiple failing scooter rivals, but was delisted from Nasdaq in 2024 amid steep losses.
lesson: Rolling up other struggling scooter companies compounds losses rather than fixing the underlying economics.

name: Homejoy
summary: On-demand home cleaning marketplace backed by Google Ventures shut down after burning through cash and facing worker-classification lawsuits.
lesson: Subsidized on-demand marketplaces can collapse quickly when customer acquisition costs outpace retention and legal risk mounts.

name: Hopin
summary: Virtual-events SaaS platform rocketed to a $7.75B valuation during the pandemic, then saw demand collapse as in-person events returned; sold its core product to RingCentral for a fraction of capital raised.
lesson: Pandemic-driven demand spikes can reverse just as fast, punishing companies that scaled to match the peak.

name: Houseparty
summary: The group video-chat app spiked during COVID lockdowns but usage collapsed once Zoom and FaceTime added similar features; Epic Games shut it down in 2021.
lesson: A pandemic-driven usage spike can mask the absence of durable competitive advantage.

name: HQ Trivia
summary: The live mobile trivia game show was a viral 2017-18 phenomenon but usage crashed after co-founder disputes and it failed to build a sustainable business, shutting down in 2020.
lesson: A live scheduled format that depends on constant novelty struggles to retain users once the fad fades.

name: Humane
summary: Ex-Apple engineers' AI Pin wearable was panned by reviewers for poor performance and battery life; the company sold its assets to HP for $116M.
lesson: Radically rethinking a product category requires the new form factor to actually outperform the old one on core tasks.

name: IBM Watson Health
summary: IBM's flagship healthcare AI division failed to deliver on promises of AI-driven cancer treatment recommendations and was sold off in pieces for about $1B.
lesson: Overpromising AI capability in high-stakes domains like healthcare invites credibility collapse when results disappoint.

name: Ike
summary: Autonomous trucking startup founded by former Google and Otto engineers that was acquired by Nuro in 2022 primarily for its engineering talent, ending as an independent company.
lesson: Talent-acquisition exits in capital-intensive AV trucking are common when independent scaling proves too costly.

name: InVision
summary: Design collaboration platform reached a $1.9B valuation but lost most of its user base to Figma's real-time collaborative editor, leading to major layoffs.
lesson: A market leader can be dethroned quickly if a competitor removes a fundamental workflow friction.

name: Invitae
summary: Genetic-testing company scaled rapidly through acquisitions to become a genomics giant but never reached profitability and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in February 2024, selling off its assets.
lesson: Rolling up acquisitions to chase scale doesn't fix a fundamentally unprofitable core business.

name: IRL
summary: The event-planning social app claimed to be the 'anti-Facebook' and reached unicorn status, then shut down in 2023 after investigations found most of its claimed users were bots.
lesson: Inflated user metrics eventually collide with reality, especially once regulators start asking questions.

name: IronNet
summary: Cybersecurity firm founded by a former NSA director went public via SPAC, then filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after failing to convert government pedigree into sustained commercial revenue.
lesson: Prestigious founders and government contacts don't guarantee a repeatable commercial sales motion.

name: Jabong
summary: Indian fashion ecommerce marketplace was sold to rival Myntra (Flipkart Group) in 2016 for roughly $70M, a steep discount to the $225M+ it had raised.
lesson: Fast-scaling fashion marketplaces in emerging markets can burn capital faster than the category can support.

name: Jawbone
summary: Consumer wearables and speaker maker raised nearly a billion dollars but couldn't compete with Fitbit and Apple Watch on quality and distribution.
lesson: Raising huge sums does not substitute for reliable hardware quality in a fast-moving consumer category.

name: Jet Airways
summary: Once India's largest private airline grounded its entire fleet under $1.2B of debt; a 2021 revival attempt also failed.
lesson: Heavily leveraged airline expansion without disciplined cost control eventually catches up with even market leaders.

name: Jet.com
summary: Membership-style ecommerce marketplace built to compete with Amazon was acquired by Walmart for $3.3B in 2016, then shut down in 2020 as Walmart consolidated its ecommerce efforts under its own brand.
lesson: Even a well-funded challenger can be absorbed and discarded once its acquirer no longer needs the separate brand.

name: Jibo
summary: Social home robot billed as 'the world's first family robot' shipped late in a market Amazon Echo and Google Home had already claimed with cheaper voice assistants.
lesson: A charming but expensive robot can be leapfrogged by cheap smart speakers solving the same core need.

name: Juicero
summary: Startup selling a $400 WiFi-connected juice press for proprietary produce packs; collapsed after reporters showed the packs could be squeezed by hand.
lesson: Over-engineering a solution to a problem customers didn't have destroys trust fast.

name: Julep
summary: Beauty subscription and D2C nail/cosmetics brand ceased operations in 2018 after its assets were sold following a failed pivot to social selling.
lesson: Pivoting a subscription brand's business model late in its life rarely reverses declining fundamentals.

name: Jump Bikes
summary: Electric bike-share startup (originally Social Bicycles) acquired by Uber in 2018; Uber shut down and sold off Jump's operations in 2020 amid COVID-driven cost cuts.
lesson: A promising acquisition can still be unwound when the parent company's own priorities shift.

name: Kabbage
summary: SMB lending fintech whose core platform was acquired by American Express in 2020; the remaining legal entity (K Servicing) filed for bankruptcy in 2022 facing lawsuits over fraudulent PPP loans it had originated.
lesson: Loosening underwriting standards to move fast during a government relief program created liability that outlived the acquired business itself.

name: Karhoo
summary: London-based ride-hailing marketplace aggregating taxis and private-hire cars that collapsed into administration in 2017 just months after a high-profile US launch.
lesson: Aggressive multi-country expansion funded by debt-like structures can implode quickly when investor confidence wavers.

name: Katapult
summary: Rent-to-own e-commerce financing platform that went public via SPAC in 2021 at a ~$1.1B valuation; stock collapsed over 95% amid weak credit quality and was later delisted from Nasdaq.
lesson: SPAC valuations built on growth projections can mask fundamentally weak lending unit economics.

name: Katerra
summary: SoftBank-backed construction technology company tried to vertically integrate design, manufacturing, and building, but collapsed into bankruptcy.
lesson: Vertically integrating a capital-intensive industrial business too fast, funded by growth-stage capital, can outrun operational competence.

name: Knewton
summary: Adaptive-learning pioneer raised huge venture rounds on the promise of AI-personalized courseware but never found a durable business model, and sold its assets to Wiley.
lesson: Being early with impressive AI technology doesn't guarantee a viable go-to-market or revenue model.

name: Knotel
summary: Flexible office space provider positioned as an enterprise-focused WeWork alternative filed for Chapter 11 after the pandemic gutted office demand.
lesson: A lease-arbitrage business model is acutely vulnerable to sudden demand shocks like a pandemic.

name: Kozmo.com
summary: Promised one-hour delivery of small goods in major cities and shut down in 2001 after the free delivery model proved impossibly expensive.
lesson: Free ultra-fast delivery for low-value orders destroys margins regardless of funding size.

name: Le Tote
summary: Clothing rental subscription service acquired ModCloth in 2019, then filed for bankruptcy in 2020, permanently closing ModCloth's remaining operations too.
lesson: Combining two struggling ecommerce businesses rarely produces a stronger combined entity.

name: Leap Transit
summary: San Francisco luxury commuter bus startup shut down by regulators in 2015 for operating without proper permits, less than a year after launch.
lesson: Skipping local transit permitting requirements to move fast can end a company almost overnight.

name: LendUp
summary: Online lender aimed at underbanked consumers that was repeatedly fined by the CFPB for deceptive practices and illegal fees, and shut down its lending operations in 2022.
lesson: Serving underbanked customers profitably requires compliance built in from day one, not bolted on after regulators intervene.

name: Letgo
summary: Secondhand goods marketplace app raised nearly $1B before merging with rival OfferUp in 2020, and the letgo brand was ultimately discontinued in 2022.
lesson: Two well-funded rivals fighting for the same local marketplace niche often end in a costly consolidation rather than a standalone win.

name: Living Social
summary: Daily deals marketplace once valued near $6B raised nearly $1B before being sold to rival Groupon for a nominal $1 in 2016.
lesson: Deals marketplaces without repeat-purchase loyalty burn through capital chasing an ever-churning customer base.

name: Living.com
summary: Online furniture retailer shut down in 2000 after failing to solve the logistics and returns costs of shipping bulky furniture.
lesson: Category economics for bulky, high-return goods make naive ecommerce models unprofitable.

name: Local Motors
summary: Pioneer of 3D-printed and open-source vehicle manufacturing, including the Olli autonomous shuttle, that abruptly shut down in 2022 after failing to close a new funding round.
lesson: Innovative manufacturing techniques alone don't create a sustainable business without a clear path to volume revenue.

name: Lordstown Motors
summary: Electric pickup truck maker went public via SPAC and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy amid fraud allegations over inflated pre-order numbers.
lesson: Inflated demand claims used to attract SPAC investors eventually collide with real production and sales numbers.

name: Luxe
summary: On-demand valet parking startup operating in several major US cities that shut down its consumer parking service in 2017, with Volvo licensing its technology afterward.
lesson: On-demand valet labor costs proved too high relative to what urban parkers were willing to pay.

name: Magic Leap
summary: Augmented reality headset maker raised over $3.5B, but the shipped product badly underdelivered versus years of hype, triggering mass layoffs and a pivot to enterprise-only sales.
lesson: Years of secretive hype-building can set expectations no shipping consumer product can realistically meet.

name: Managed by Q
summary: Office management and facilities-services marketplace acquired by WeWork; operations were wound down as WeWork retrenched during its own crisis.
lesson: An acquisition by a troubled parent company can doom an otherwise-functioning business.

name: Maple
summary: NYC-based prepared meal delivery startup co-founded by a celebrity chef shut down in 2017, with its team acquired by Deliveroo.
lesson: A crowded meal-delivery market punishes startups that can't reach delivery density fast enough.

name: MasterClass
summary: Celebrity-taught online course platform saw sharp subscriber growth during the pandemic reverse afterward, leading to layoffs and a roughly 75% internal valuation markdown.
lesson: Pandemic-boosted consumer subscription demand for enrichment content proved largely temporary.

name: Maven
summary: General Motors' car-sharing brand operating in over a dozen US cities was shut down in 2019 as GM redirected investment toward Cruise's autonomous vehicle efforts.
lesson: Corporate mobility experiments get cut quickly when a flashier bet (like autonomy) captures leadership's attention.

name: MedMen
summary: Cannabis retail chain that branded itself as the 'Apple Store of weed' expanded aggressively across multiple states, then saw its stock collapse over 99% and default on debt.
lesson: Aggressive multi-state retail expansion can collapse fast when unit economics never catch up to store count.

name: Meerkat
summary: The live video-streaming app rode SXSW buzz but was crushed within weeks after Twitter cut off its social-graph access and launched rival Periscope.
lesson: Building a growth engine on top of a rival platform's API leaves you exposed to a single policy change.

name: Metromile
summary: Pay-per-mile auto insurance startup that went public via SPAC in 2021 and was acquired by Lemonade in an all-stock deal in 2022 at a steep discount to its earlier valuation, after failing to reach profitable scale.
lesson: A distressed-sale acquisition can be the realistic best outcome once a capital-intensive insurtech's growth-at-a-loss model runs out of runway.

name: Mixer
summary: Microsoft's live-streaming platform never closed the gap with Twitch despite exclusive deals with stars like Ninja, and was shut down in 2020.
lesson: Buying exclusive star creators can't overcome an entrenched platform's network effects and habit formation.

name: ModCloth
summary: Indie fashion ecommerce retailer sold to Walmart in 2017 for a fraction of its funding, then resold to Go Global Retail in 2019 before winding down meaningful operations.
lesson: Acquisition by a larger retailer isn't a rescue if the underlying economics never improve.

name: Modsy
summary: 3D interior design and furniture ecommerce startup shut down in 2022 after failing to convert design engagement into sustainable furniture sales margins.
lesson: Rich design experiences don't guarantee conversion rates high enough to cover furniture ecommerce costs.

name: Mt. Gox
summary: Once handling over 70% of all bitcoin transactions, the Tokyo exchange collapsed after admitting roughly 850,000 BTC had been lost or stolen through years of undetected hacking.
lesson: Exchanges holding customer assets need auditable, professional-grade security from day one.

name: Munchery
summary: On-demand prepared meal delivery startup shut down abruptly in 2019, leaving customers and vendors unpaid.
lesson: Food delivery logistics have thin margins that heavy funding cannot mask indefinitely.

name: Myspace
summary: The dominant social network of the mid-2000s lost its lead to Facebook after clumsy redesigns, ad clutter, and management turmoil under News Corp ownership.
lesson: Acquisition by a media conglomerate can smother the product agility that made a startup win.

name: Namely
summary: HR and payroll software for mid-sized companies raised over $260M before being sold to Vensure Employer Services after struggling to grow into its valuation.
lesson: Raising at aggressive valuations can force an eventual down-market exit if growth doesn't keep pace.

name: Napster
summary: The pioneering peer-to-peer music-sharing service was shut down by court injunction in 2001-2002 after a landmark RIAA copyright infringement lawsuit.
lesson: A product built on unresolved legal liability can be shut down overnight regardless of user love.

name: Nasty Gal
summary: Vintage-turned-fast-fashion ecommerce retailer filed for bankruptcy in 2016 and was acquired out of bankruptcy by Boohoo.
lesson: Rapid scaling of headcount and inventory without matching operational discipline can sink even a hot brand.

name: Nikola Corporation
summary: Hydrogen and electric truck maker went public via SPAC at an eye-popping valuation, was found by short-sellers to have faked a demo video, and filed for bankruptcy in 2025.
lesson: Fabricating product demonstrations to inflate a valuation is a criminal liability, not just a PR risk.

name: Nurx
summary: Direct-to-consumer telehealth startup for birth control and sexual health prescriptions was merged into Thirty Madison in 2022, with the standalone Nurx brand discontinued the following year after regulatory scrutiny over prescribing practices.
lesson: Consolidation is often the endpoint for DTC telehealth brands that can't sustain independent scale.

name: Oberlo
summary: Dropshipping tool that let Shopify merchants import products from AliExpress was acquired by Shopify in 2017 and discontinued in 2022 as Shopify shifted strategy toward other fulfillment partners.
lesson: Even category-defining tools can be discontinued once the parent platform's strategic priorities shift.

name: Ofo
summary: Chinese dockless bike-share giant that expanded to 20+ countries but collapsed under debt, deposit-refund scandals, and cutthroat competition with Mobike, retreating from most markets by 2019.
lesson: Blitzscaling into dozens of countries simultaneously without a path to profitability invites collapse.

name: Olive AI
summary: Healthcare AI automation startup once valued at $4B shut down and sold off business units after burning through nearly a billion dollars without delivering on its broad AI-automation promises.
lesson: Trying to automate too many disparate workflows at once can prevent an AI startup from proving value in any single one.

name: One Kings Lane
summary: Flash-sale home decor ecommerce site sold to Bed Bath & Beyond in a 2016 fire sale after raising over $200M, and its ecommerce operations were later wound down.
lesson: Flash-sale economics erode once novelty fades and customer acquisition costs rise.

name: Outdoor Voices
summary: Athleisure D2C brand once valued near $400M was sold in a distressed deal in 2023 after years of executive turnover and cash problems.
lesson: Retail store overexpansion and leadership churn can unravel a hot D2C brand's momentum quickly.

name: Ozy Media
summary: The digital media startup collapsed within weeks in 2021 after a New York Times investigation and a fraudulent investor conference call exposed inflated traffic and revenue claims.
lesson: Fabricating metrics to investors is an existential risk, not a growth hack.

name: Parler
summary: The 'free speech' social network lost hosting and app-store access after the January 6, 2021 Capitol riot, and never recovered its user base.
lesson: Dependence on a handful of infrastructure and distribution gatekeepers is an existential single point of failure.

name: Path
summary: Intimate 'close friends' mobile social network never reached scale against Facebook; sold to Daum Kakao in 2015 and shut down in 2018.
lesson: A beautifully designed niche product can still lose to the incumbent's network effects.

name: Peapod
summary: One of the earliest online grocery delivery services, owned by Ahold Delhaize, was shut down in most U.S. markets in 2021 amid competition from Instacart and retailer-owned delivery.
lesson: First-mover advantage in ecommerce grocery erodes if the technology platform isn't continually modernized.

name: Pear Therapeutics
summary: Pioneer of FDA-cleared prescription digital therapeutics for addiction and insomnia went public via SPAC but filed for bankruptcy in 2023 after failing to secure adequate insurance reimbursement.
lesson: Prescription digital therapeutics need a proven reimbursement pathway before scaling burn.

name: Percolate
summary: Content marketing and brand management SaaS raised over $135M but was sold to Seismic in what was widely reported as a distressed, down-round acquisition.
lesson: Large enterprise contracts don't guarantee unit economics that support the cash burned to win them.

name: Petal
summary: Credit-card fintech using cash-flow underwriting to serve credit-invisible consumers; sold its card portfolio to Prosper in 2023 and wound down its core consumer card-issuing business after failing to reach profitability.
lesson: Alternative-underwriting credit products still require balance-sheet scale and cheap funding that early-stage fintechs struggle to secure.

name: Pets.com
summary: Online pet supplies retailer famous for its sock-puppet mascot burned through IPO proceeds on marketing and cheap shipping of heavy, low-margin goods before closing in 2000.
lesson: Marketing spend cannot compensate for a fundamentally unprofitable shipping and margin structure.

name: Plastiq
summary: Bill-payment platform that let businesses pay non-card-accepting vendors with a credit card; filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in May 2023 after burning through venture funding without a durable margin.
lesson: Interchange-fee arbitrage business models are vulnerable when partner banks or card networks tighten terms.

name: Plenty
summary: SoftBank-backed indoor vertical farming company filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after years of losses growing leafy greens indoors at high energy cost.
lesson: Massive venture funding can delay but not solve a fundamentally challenging cost structure in indoor agriculture.

name: Prosper Marketplace
summary: One of the original P2P lending marketplaces that underwent a major crisis in 2016 with mass layoffs and investor pullback, and has operated at sharply reduced scale in the years since.
lesson: Marketplace lenders depend on institutional investor confidence in loan quality, which can evaporate industry-wide after a single peer's scandal.

name: Proterra
summary: Electric bus and battery maker that went public via SPAC in 2021 but filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023 due to rising costs, supply chain issues, and cash burn, later selling its bus unit to Phoenix Motor.
lesson: Even a well-regarded electric bus product with real transit customers can be undone by manufacturing cost overruns.

name: Proteus Digital Health
summary: Pioneer of ingestible 'digital pill' sensors that tracked medication adherence failed to find commercial traction with payers and providers and filed for bankruptcy in 2020.
lesson: A technically impressive product still needs a clear reimbursement path to become a business.

name: Quartet Health
summary: Mental-health referral and care-coordination platform that connected primary care patients to behavioral health providers shut down operations in 2023 after failing to reach profitability.
lesson: Coordination platforms need a durable revenue model beyond thin per-referral fees.

name: Quibi
summary: Jeffrey Katzenberg and Meg Whitman's short-form premium streaming service raised $1.75B for mobile-only shows but shut down just six months after launch amid the pandemic and weak demand.
lesson: Massive funding and star power cannot substitute for validating that people actually want the format.

name: Quidsi (Diapers.com)
summary: Diapers.com and related sites were acquired by Amazon in 2011 for $545M after a fierce price war, then shut down entirely by Amazon in 2017.
lesson: Competing on price against Amazon in its own category is a losing long-term strategy even with an acquisition exit.

name: Quirky
summary: Crowdsourced invention marketplace that manufactured and sold community-designed products filed for bankruptcy in 2015 after overexpanding into hardware and smart-home devices.
lesson: Manufacturing physical inventory at scale requires far more capital discipline than a software marketplace.

name: Quixey
summary: Mobile app search engine raised over $200M including from SoftBank, but shut down amid allegations that executives had misrepresented revenue figures to investors.
lesson: Inflating metrics to secure large late-stage rounds eventually catches up with a company.

name: ReachNow
summary: BMW's US car-sharing and ride-hailing service launched in Seattle and other cities but was shut down in 2019 as BMW consolidated mobility efforts into Share Now.
lesson: Automaker mobility spinoffs often get folded or killed when parent-company strategy consolidates.

name: REEF Technology
summary: Parking-lot-turned-logistics-hub company (originally ParkJockey) that raised a huge SoftBank-led round at a $12.6B valuation but sold off major parking and delivery-kitchen units in 2023 amid deep losses.
lesson: Pivoting a parking-lot network into multiple unrelated logistics businesses simultaneously diluted focus and burned cash.

name: Rethink Robotics
summary: Maker of the Baxter and Sawyer collaborative industrial robots shut down after failing to find enough paying manufacturing customers.
lesson: Being technically groundbreaking in industrial robotics isn't sufficient if factory customers aren't yet ready to adopt at scale.

name: Root Insurance
summary: Usage-based car insurance startup that IPO'd in 2020 at a multi-billion-dollar valuation; its stock has since fallen more than 95% amid persistent underwriting losses and high customer acquisition costs.
lesson: Telematics-based pricing didn't solve insurtech's fundamental problem: acquiring and retaining customers profitably in a thin-margin, heavily regulated line of business.

name: ScaleFactor
summary: 'AI-powered' small-business bookkeeping SaaS shut down after a Forbes investigation revealed its automation was largely manual human bookkeeping that frequently produced errors.
lesson: Marketing a product as AI-automated when it isn't reliably working can destroy trust once customers are exposed to the failures.

name: Secret
summary: Anonymous confession-sharing app raised heavily on a viral launch but was shut down by founders after becoming a magnet for bullying and harassment.
lesson: Founders should be willing to shut down a growing product if it can't be made safe.

name: Selina
summary: Latin America-born hostel/co-living hospitality brand expanded to 150+ locations, went public via SPAC, and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy amid unsustainable lease obligations.
lesson: Rapid global real-estate expansion without proving profitability at existing locations first is a recurring hospitality failure pattern.

name: Shift Technologies
summary: Online used car buying and selling marketplace went public via SPAC in 2020 and wound down operations in 2023 after years of heavy losses.
lesson: Online used-car marketplaces face brutal working-capital and reconditioning costs that scale with volume.

name: ShopClues
summary: Indian online marketplace for unbranded and discounted goods, once valued over $1B, was sold in a distressed 2019 deal for a small fraction of capital raised.
lesson: Marketplaces built around counterfeit-prone, low-trust categories struggle to sustain premium valuations.

name: Shuddle
summary: 'Uber for kids' ride-hailing startup providing vetted drivers for unaccompanied minors that shut down in 2016 after failing to raise a follow-on funding round.
lesson: Trust-and-safety-heavy niches like child transportation require expensive vetting infrastructure that's hard to fund at startup speed.

name: Shyp
summary: On-demand package pickup and shipping startup shut down in 2018 after failing to find a scalable, profitable model.
lesson: Convenience-as-a-service businesses need pricing that actually covers labor and logistics costs.

name: Sidecar
summary: Early ride-hailing and courier pioneer that introduced features later copied by Uber and Lyft but shut down its ride service in 2015, selling assets to General Motors.
lesson: Being first with an innovative feature doesn't matter if better-funded competitors can copy it and outspend you.

name: Signature Bank
summary: New York bank with a large crypto and digital-asset deposit base that was closed by regulators in March 2023 amid contagion from Silicon Valley Bank's collapse and a rapid depositor run.
lesson: Uninsured deposit concentration can turn a solvent-on-paper bank into a forced closure within days once confidence breaks.

name: Silicon Valley Bank
summary: Bank central to the startup ecosystem that failed in March 2023 after rising rates devalued its bond portfolio and a concentrated, tech-sector depositor base fled within 48 hours.
lesson: A depositor base concentrated in one interconnected industry can coordinate a bank run faster than traditional liquidity risk models assume.

name: Silvercar
summary: Premium airport rental-car startup (all-silver Audi A4 fleet) that Audi acquired in 2017 for an estimated $300M+, then shut down entirely in 2019 as Audi refocused its mobility strategy.
lesson: A lucrative acquisition exit doesn't guarantee long-term survival once the acquirer's strategic priorities change.

name: Silvergate Bank
summary: California bank that pivoted to become the primary banking rail for crypto exchanges; collapsed in March 2023 after FTX's failure triggered a deposit run it could not cover without fire-selling securities.
lesson: Concentrating deposits in one volatile client sector creates correlated withdrawal risk that ordinary liquidity buffers cannot absorb.

name: Simple
summary: One of the earliest US neobanks, acquired by BBVA in 2014 for $117M; shut down in 2021 after PNC acquired BBVA USA and decided to consolidate customers onto its own digital platform.
lesson: Being an acquisition pioneer doesn't guarantee survival once the acquirer itself gets acquired.

name: Skip Scooters
summary: San Francisco-based e-scooter operator that lost several city permits and was later acquired by Helbiz, which itself unwound the business.
lesson: Permit-dependent micromobility businesses live and die by city regulatory relationships.

name: Slync
summary: Freight and supply-chain orchestration software startup that shut down in 2023 after its founder and CEO were criminally charged with defrauding investors.
lesson: Founder fraud can vaporize a well-funded logistics-tech company overnight, regardless of the underlying product.

name: SmileDirectClub
summary: Direct-to-consumer clear-aligner company that bypassed in-person orthodontists faced lawsuits over safety and regulatory pushback from dentists, and shut down entirely in December 2023 after bankruptcy.
lesson: Disintermediating licensed clinicians invites regulatory and legal resistance that can outlast the growth story.

name: Snapdeal
summary: Indian ecommerce marketplace once valued near $6.5B saw a collapsed merger attempt with Flipkart in 2017, followed by mass layoffs and a drastic valuation markdown, and now operates as a much smaller value-fashion platform.
lesson: Overexpanding a marketplace ahead of proven unit economics leaves no cushion when a funding-driven price war arrives.

name: Solyndra
summary: Solar panel maker using cylindrical thin-film tubes, backed by a $535M DOE loan guarantee, collapsed when Chinese silicon panel prices crashed and undercut its cost structure.
lesson: A capital-intensive manufacturing bet can be wiped out by a commodity price collapse it has no control over.

name: Sonder Holdings
summary: Tech-enabled short-term rental operator went public via SPAC, saw a proposed Marriott partnership collapse, and wound down operations in 2025.
lesson: Leasing large real-estate portfolios to run a hospitality brand requires far deeper capital reserves than software-style startups assume.

name: Spin
summary: E-scooter startup acquired by Ford in 2018, then sold to Tier Mobility in 2022 after Ford failed to make micromobility work within its business.
lesson: Corporate-owned mobility units often get deprioritized and divested when parent strategy shifts.

name: Spirit Airlines
summary: US ultra-low-cost carrier filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after a proposed JetBlue merger was blocked by regulators and it couldn't return to profitability post-COVID.
lesson: A blocked exit can leave a structurally unprofitable business with no path forward but bankruptcy.

name: SpoonRocket
summary: Instant meal delivery startup promising 10-minute delivery shut down in 2016 after running out of funding.
lesson: Extreme delivery-speed promises multiply operating costs beyond what thin food margins can support.

name: Sprig
summary: On-demand healthy meal delivery service shut down in 2017 after failing to make its kitchen-to-door model profitable.
lesson: Vertically integrated food delivery requires enormous capital to reach the density needed for profitability.

name: Spring (Shop Spring)
summary: Mobile shopping marketplace app aggregating brands into one checkout experience shut down in 2017 after failing to gain enough traction against larger platforms.
lesson: Aggregator marketplaces need a real distribution edge, not just a better checkout UX.

name: Stability AI
summary: Open-source generative AI company (Stable Diffusion) faced severe cash-flow problems, unpaid compute bills, and a wave of researcher departures, culminating in CEO Emad Mostaque's resignation in 2024.
lesson: Open-source AI model releases build brand but not necessarily a monetization engine to fund the compute costs behind them.

name: Starsky Robotics
summary: Autonomous trucking startup that achieved a driverless highway run in 2019 but shut down in 2020 after failing to raise a new funding round, citing investor skepticism toward pure self-driving plays.
lesson: Technical milestones don't guarantee fundability when investor sentiment toward a whole subsector sours.

name: Stitch Labs
summary: Inventory and order management software for ecommerce sellers was acquired by Square in 2019 and discontinued in 2020 as Square consolidated its commerce tools.
lesson: Being acquired doesn't guarantee product continuity if the acquirer's roadmap diverges.

name: Summon
summary: Los Angeles-based ride-hailing startup that attempted to compete directly with Uber and Lyft in a single metro market and shut down within two years.
lesson: Regional ride-hailing challengers rarely survive head-on competition against nationally-scaled incumbents.

name: Sun Basket
summary: Organic and specialty-diet meal kit subscription service underwent massive layoffs and downsizing starting in 2022 as investor funding for meal kits dried up.
lesson: Premium positioning doesn't exempt meal-kit subscriptions from the category's structural retention and logistics costs.

name: Superpedestrian
summary: MIT-spinout maker of the LINK scooter with proprietary safety tech that shut down its micromobility operations in late 2023 after failing to raise further funding.
lesson: Even differentiated hardware-safety technology couldn't overcome the capital intensity of scooter-fleet operations.

name: Synapse Financial Technologies
summary: Banking-as-a-service middleware provider that collapsed into bankruptcy in 2024, leaving reconciliation discrepancies between partner banks and fintech apps that froze an estimated $85M+ in end-customer deposits.
lesson: In layered banking-as-a-service stacks, poor ledger reconciliation between middleware and partner banks can strand real customer money.

name: TAL Education Group
summary: Publicly traded Chinese tutoring giant's stock collapsed over 90% after Beijing banned for-profit tutoring in core school subjects.
lesson: A single sweeping regulatory policy change can instantly dismantle an entire national industry segment.

name: Terraform Labs
summary: Creator of the TerraUSD algorithmic stablecoin and LUNA token; the ecosystem collapsed in May 2022 when UST lost its dollar peg, erasing roughly $40B in value within days.
lesson: An algorithmic stablecoin backed only by a sister token's market confidence has no real collateral floor.

name: Terraform Labs (Terra/Luna)
summary: Creator of the algorithmic stablecoin UST and sister token LUNA, which de-pegged and collapsed in May 2022, wiping out roughly $40B in value; the company filed for bankruptcy in 2024 and its founder was later found liable for fraud.
lesson: Algorithmic stablecoins backed by a reflexive token rather than real reserves are structurally vulnerable to bank-run dynamics.

name: The Iron Yard
summary: One of the largest US coding bootcamp networks with 15+ campuses shut down abruptly after parent Apollo Education Group failed to find a buyer.
lesson: Fast multi-city expansion outpaced sustainable unit economics for coding bootcamps.

name: The Yes
summary: AI-powered personalized fashion shopping app was acquired by Pinterest in 2022 for its technology and talent, then shut down in 2023.
lesson: An acqui-hire exit for the team doesn't mean the product itself found lasting product-market fit.

name: Theranos
summary: Claimed its Edison hardware could run hundreds of blood tests from a finger-prick, but the technology never worked as advertised; founder was later convicted.
lesson: Deep tech claims that defy scientific consensus require independent verification before they can be trusted.

name: Thomas Cook
summary: Britain's oldest travel agent collapsed into compulsory liquidation in 2019 under £1.7B of debt, triggering the largest peacetime repatriation in UK history.
lesson: Legacy travel giants weighed down by debt and physical infrastructure can be wiped out fast by shifting booking habits.

name: Three Arrows Capital
summary: Singapore-based crypto hedge fund ordered into liquidation after over-leveraged bets, including a large Terra/LUNA position, wiped out its capital.
lesson: Leverage on illiquid, correlated crypto bets turns a single bad trade into systemic contagion.

name: Tier Mobility
summary: European scooter and e-bike sharing leader that acquired Spin in 2022 but filed for insolvency proceedings in 2024 after failing to reach profitability at scale.
lesson: Market leadership in micromobility doesn't guarantee profitability when vehicle replacement costs stay high.

name: Totsy
summary: Flash-sale ecommerce site for baby and kids products shut down in 2013 as the flash-sale category became oversaturated.
lesson: Entering a flash-sale niche late, after category leaders are entrenched, leaves little room to build differentiation.

name: Tradeshift
summary: B2B procurement and supply-chain platform once valued near $2.7B ran into severe financial distress, restructuring and selling off assets after years of heavy losses.
lesson: Raising enormous sums for a broad procurement platform vision without matching revenue can lead to a slow-motion collapse even at unicorn scale.

name: Trunk Club
summary: Personal styling and menswear ecommerce service was acquired by Nordstrom for $350M in 2014 but was shut down in 2022 after years of losses.
lesson: High-touch personal styling has cost structures that are hard to scale profitably even under a retail parent.

name: Tumblr
summary: Once bought by Yahoo for $1.1 billion, the blogging platform was mismanaged under Yahoo/Verizon ownership and a 2018 adult-content ban drove users away, ending in a sale to Automattic for ~$3M.
lesson: A large acquisition price can be destroyed by years of neglect and a single poorly executed content policy change.

name: TuSimple
summary: Autonomous trucking company that IPO'd in 2021 but wound down its US trucking operations in 2024 amid a federal fraud investigation.
lesson: Governance scandals can destroy an AV company's public-market credibility even after a successful IPO.

name: uBiome
summary: Microbiome-testing startup was raided by the FBI over billing practices for its gut-health tests and filed for bankruptcy months later amid fraud allegations against its founders.
lesson: Aggressive insurance billing practices in consumer diagnostics invite regulatory scrutiny that can end a company overnight.

name: Vacasa
summary: Largest US vacation rental property manager went public via SPAC, lost over 95% of its stock value amid heavy losses, and was taken private by Casago.
lesson: Rapid roll-up growth in property management can mask deteriorating per-unit profitability until public markets expose it.

name: Value America
summary: Early online superstore that IPO'd in 1999 collapsed in 2000 amid fulfillment failures and executive turmoil.
lesson: Ecommerce breadth without reliable fulfillment infrastructure destroys customer trust fast.

name: Vessel
summary: A premium early-access video subscription service for YouTube creators struggled to gain traction and was acquired and dissolved by Verizon within two years.
lesson: Paying creators for exclusivity windows is hard to sustain when the free version is one tab away.

name: Vice Media
summary: Once valued near $6B, the youth media empire filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023 after years of missed revenue targets, mass layoffs, and an unsustainable ad-and-studio model.
lesson: A soaring private valuation is not a substitute for a profitable core business.

name: Vine
summary: Short-form looping video pioneer acquired by Twitter before launch; failed to build creator monetization and was shut down in 2016-17 as Instagram and Snapchat surged.
lesson: Owning a creative format isn't enough if you never build a way for creators to earn on it.

name: Virgin Hyperloop
summary: Richard Branson-backed hyperloop startup that ran the first passenger hyperloop test in 2020 but pivoted away from passenger transport and laid off half its staff in 2022, effectively ending its original mission.
lesson: Novel transportation modes face such steep regulatory and infrastructure hurdles that even successful tech demos don't guarantee commercialization.

name: Virgin Orbit
summary: Richard Branson's air-launched small-satellite rocket company filed for bankruptcy after a high-profile UK launch failure and inability to raise further funding.
lesson: A single high-visibility launch failure can freeze funding for a capital-intensive space startup with thin cash reserves.

name: Vogo
summary: Bangalore-based dockless scooter rental startup backed by Ola that wound down operations in 2022 as parent Ola restructured its mobility investments.
lesson: A startup dependent on a single strategic investor is vulnerable to that investor's own portfolio triage decisions.

name: Volt Bank
summary: Australia's first licensed neobank, which returned customer deposits and handed back its banking license in mid-2022 after a planned $100M capital raise fell through.
lesson: Regulatory capital requirements mean a neobank's survival can hinge entirely on one funding round closing on time.

name: Volta Trucks
summary: Swedish electric commercial truck maker that filed for insolvency in late 2023 after its battery supplier Proterra went bankrupt, disrupting Volta's own production ramp.
lesson: Supply-chain dependency risk cascades: one supplier's bankruptcy can trigger a customer's collapse too.

name: Voyage
summary: Self-driving taxi startup focused on retirement communities that was acquired by Cruise in 2021, ending its run as an independent AV company.
lesson: Niche AV deployment strategies (like retirement communities) can prove a concept but rarely generate independent scale.

name: Voyager Digital
summary: Crypto brokerage and lender filed for Chapter 11 after Three Arrows Capital defaulted on a $650M loan, leaving a large hole in its balance sheet.
lesson: Concentrated counterparty exposure to a single hedge fund can sink an otherwise-operating lending business overnight.

name: Vroom
summary: Online used car retailer IPO'd in 2020 at a multi-billion dollar valuation but wound down its ecommerce car-buying operations in 2022 after mounting losses, pivoting to a finance-only company.
lesson: Even well-funded, high-profile IPOs can't outrun negative unit economics in capital-intensive categories.

name: Wanelo
summary: Social shopping app that let users browse and save products from online stores shut down in 2019 after losing relevance to Instagram and Pinterest shopping features.
lesson: Standalone social-shopping apps struggle once platform giants add native shopping features.

name: Webvan
summary: Dot-com era online grocery delivery company built massive automated warehouses ahead of demand and filed for bankruptcy in 2001, becoming a canonical dot-com bust.
lesson: Building expensive infrastructure years ahead of proven consumer demand is a fatal capital allocation mistake.

name: WeWork
summary: Coworking company that once reached a $47B private valuation filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after years of unsustainable long-term lease liabilities and a failed 2019 IPO.
lesson: Locking in long-term lease liabilities while selling short-term memberships creates a fatal duration mismatch in a downturn.

name: Wheels
summary: Seated e-moped/scooter startup that was acquired by Helbiz in 2022 as the broader micromobility sector consolidated amid financing pressure.
lesson: An oversaturated scooter market forced consolidation among undifferentiated players.

name: Winc
summary: Wine subscription and D2C ecommerce company (formerly Club W) went public via SPAC and filed for bankruptcy in 2024 amid falling subscriber counts.
lesson: Subscription commerce for discretionary categories is vulnerable to churn once novelty fades.

name: Wirecard
summary: German payment processor that fabricated €1.9B in nonexistent cash balances; collapsed into insolvency in 2020 after auditors and journalists exposed the fraud.
lesson: Audited financials and regulatory sign-off are not proof of legitimacy when a company controls its own auditors and regulators.

name: Wish (ContextLogic)
summary: Discount marketplace app connecting shoppers to Chinese sellers IPO'd in 2020 at a ~$14B valuation, then collapsed as user growth reversed and was sold to Qoo10 in 2024 for about $173M.
lesson: A marketplace built on cheap, slow-shipped goods struggles to retain users once competitors offer better experiences.

name: Wonga
summary: UK payday lender once valued over £1B that collapsed into administration in August 2018, overwhelmed by a wave of customer compensation claims following a regulatory crackdown on payday lending.
lesson: A high-margin lending model built on regulatory gaps collapses once those gaps close and historical customers can claim redress retroactively.

name: WOW air
summary: Icelandic ultra-low-cost long-haul carrier grew rapidly on cheap transatlantic fares but ran out of cash and ceased operations.
lesson: Ultra-low fares built on unsustainable unit economics collapse the moment fuel costs or funding tighten.

name: Xinja
summary: Australian neobank that launched with a licensed savings account offering a high interest rate; surrendered its banking license and returned deposits in December 2020 after failing to raise enough capital to sustain operations.
